one_line_takeaway: A theory paper that defines and cryptographically constructs 'unforgeable' and 'recoverable' LLM watermarking (via a new robust-digital-signature primitive built from property-preserving hashes), closing the false-attribution gap left by prior soundness-only watermarking schemes, for the Hamming-distance setting, with no empirical evaluation.
key_finding: The main technical results are two general composition theorems, not measured numbers. Theorem 1.8/5.8: given any strongly-UF-CMA signature scheme (signature size l) and any Phi-property-preserving hash (hash size k), there exists a Phi-robust digital signature scheme with signature size k+l, which is additionally Phi-recoverable if the PPH has difference recovery. Theorem 1.5/6.1/6.2: given a Phi-robust block-steganography scheme for a language model Q with block size n and a Phi-robust digital signature scheme, there exists a watermarking scheme for Q that is (secret- or public-key, matching whichever the steganography scheme uses) robust and unforgeable with respect to the every-block-close lifting EBC[Phi,n], and recoverable if the signature scheme is recoverable, provided the adversary's forged output is sufficiently long (footnote 7). Instantiating with the Hamming predicate (Corollaries 6.6/6.7) yields the first watermarking schemes -- both secret-key (under subexponential LPN hardness) and public-key (in the random oracle model) -- proved simultaneously undetectable, robust, unforgeable, and recoverable against a constant fraction of Hamming substitution errors. The paper also proves (Proposition 5.5) that difference recovery is not an extra assumption for the Hamming predicate specifically: every PPH for the Hamming predicate must satisfy it. A conceptual finding in the Discussion section contrasts this positively with Fairoze et al. 2025 (image watermarking), who showed that a natural embedding-based approach to robust+unforgeable post-hoc image watermarking becomes vacuous in practice because real embedding functions (e.g., deep-network forward passes) fail to preserve the required similarity property empirically; the authors argue their PPH-based approach for text sidesteps this because PPH adaptive security is a cryptographic guarantee rather than an empirically-checked heuristic property of a learned embedding.
core_contribution: The paper (Lin, Shahabi, Song; UW / U Chicago, Feb 2026) is a purely theoretical cryptography paper that strengthens the security guarantees of LLM watermarking. It introduces two new formal properties beyond the standard robustness/soundness/undetectability trio: (1) unforgeability, which prevents an adversary with oracle access to the watermarking algorithm (and the verification key/oracle) from producing text that is far (under a closeness predicate Phi) from every observed watermarked response yet still verifies as watermarked; and (2) recoverability, where a new algorithm Rec, given flagged text, outputs the actual substring of the original watermarked response it was copied from, and unforgeable recoverability additionally guarantees every recovered substring genuinely came from the source model. To realize these definitions the authors define a new primitive, robust (and recoverable) digital signatures -- signatures that verify on any message Phi-close to a previously signed one but cannot be forged on messages Phi-far from all signed messages -- and construct them generically from (a) any strongly UF-CMA digital signature scheme and (b) a Phi-property-preserving hash (PPH) family (Boyle-LaVigne-Vaikuntanathan, ITCS 2019), showing recoverability follows if the PPH additionally has a newly identified 'difference recovery' property (which the paper proves every Hamming PPH must satisfy). They then compose these robust signatures with an abstraction they call robust block steganography (embedding messages into fixed-size blocks of model output while preserving undetectability) to obtain a general watermarking framework (Theorem 6.1/6.2): given a Phi-robust block-steganography scheme for a language model and a Phi-robust digital signature scheme, one obtains a watermarking scheme that is robust and unforgeable with respect to the 'every-block-close' (EBC) lifting of Phi, and recoverable if the signature scheme is recoverable. They instantiate this framework for the Hamming predicate (bounded fraction of substitution errors), combining prior block-steganography constructions (Christ-Gunn 2024 secret-key scheme from ideal pseudorandom codes/LPN hardness; Fairoze et al. 2025 public-key scheme in the random oracle model) with their own robust/recoverable Hamming signature (built from a collision-resistant hash function plus a difference-recovering Hamming PPH, e.g. Holmgren et al. 2022), yielding both secret-key and public-key watermarking schemes that are simultaneously undetectable, robust, unforgeable, and recoverable against a constant fraction of Hamming substitution errors (Corollaries 6.6/6.7). The entire paper is definitions-and-proofs; there is no empirical implementation or evaluation.
threat_model: Cryptographic game-based security definitions, not an empirical attack study. Three adversary classes are formalized, all as PPT algorithms with oracle access: (1) a robustness adversary that queries the watermarking oracle Wat_sk adaptively and additionally has full access to the verification key vk (public-key setting) or oracle access to Ver_vk (secret-key setting), and tries to produce text close to a watermarked output that nonetheless fails verification (a false negative / watermark-removal attack); (2) a soundness adversary that acts independently of (sk, vk) -- i.e., without any oracle access at all -- and tries to produce text that verifies as watermarked despite never having seen watermarked output (an information-theoretic false-positive attacker, e.g. an honest human writer); (3) the paper's new unforgeability adversary, strictly stronger than the soundness adversary, which also gets the watermarking oracle Wat_sk plus vk/Ver_vk access, and tries to produce text that is 'far' from everything it has observed yet still verifies -- modeling an attacker (or a distillation/memorization process) that has seen genuine watermarked outputs or detection results and tries to frame the model owner or falsely claim/deny attribution. Stated real-world motivations include framing a model owner for harmful content, disputing authorship/ownership of AI-generated art, protecting honest users whose heavily-edited or coincidentally-correlated text might otherwise trigger a false positive, and providing 'strong evidence' for model-distillation claims (the paper explicitly cites the DeepSeek/GPT-4 distillation allegations as a motivating example). Recoverability adds a distinct threat concern: false attribution in the recovered content itself, addressed by 'unforgeable recoverability' guaranteeing every string Rec outputs was genuinely produced (approximately) by the watermarked model.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) The concrete instantiation only covers the Hamming predicate (bounded-fraction substitution/edit-position errors), which the authors themselves state 'may not capture a wide class of watermark removal attacks' -- insertions/deletions (true edit distance) are left as future work, though they sketch a path via a 'GapEdit' predicate and secure sketches for edit distance. (2) There is a structural asymmetry between the robustness guarantee (all r candidate blocks must be close to a genuine response) and the unforgeability guarantee (only the first r-1 blocks are pinned down), which the authors flag as an open gap requiring 'future strategies for embedding messages into AI-generated content' to close (Remark 1.6). (3) The framework only supports ad-hoc steganography (message embedded during generation); applying it to post-hoc watermarking (e.g. Google's SynthID-style schemes that modify already-generated content) would lose some robustness because the signature is computed over the pre-modification text while verification operates on the post-processed, further-perturbed text. (4) Extending the PPH approach to image watermarking is discussed but judged impractical with current constructions: Hamming PPHs on raw pixel bits are a poor proxy for visual similarity, and PPHs for L2 distance (via the 'contracting hypergrid vector' assumption of Bogdanov et al. 2025) require coordinate bounds (e.g. 256 for 8-bit pixels) that the authors call likely impractical. (5) No empirical validation whatsoever -- all guarantees are asymptotic/negligible-probability statements under standard or newly-proposed cryptographic assumptions (strong UF-CMA signatures, collision-resistant hashes, subexponential LPN hardness for ideal PRCs, random-oracle-model constructions), with no implementation, runtime, signature-size-in-practice, or watermark-detectability-in-practice measurements reported.

baselines_compared: Christ and Gunn (2024) secret-key undetectable watermarking / block steganography, robust to a constant fraction of Hamming errors under ideal pseudorandom codes (Alrabiah et al. 2024) -- used as a component, and characterized in Related Work as achieving unforgeability but NOT robustness when signatures are naively embedded

Fairoze et al. (2025) public-key steganography/watermarking scheme for high-entropy language models in the random oracle model, robust to a subconstant fraction of Hamming errors -- used as a component; also characterized as unforgeable-but-not-robust in its original digital-signature-based form

Golowich and Moitra (2024) pseudorandom codes robust to a constant fraction of edits (substitutions, insertions, deletions) -- discussed in Related Work as providing edit-distance robustness but weaker (non-adaptive) soundness, not compared quantitatively

Alrabiah et al. (2024) 'ideal' pseudorandom codes robust to adaptive corruption -- discussed and shown (Discussion section) to NOT by itself yield unforgeable watermarking for general (non-uniform) language models, due to embedding errors

Fairoze et al. (2025) post-hoc image watermarking (robust + unforgeable via embedding functions) -- discussed as a cautionary contrast: their embedding-based unforgeability guarantee becomes vacuous empirically because real similarity-preserving embeddings (e.g., deep network forward passes) admit adversarial near-collisions; the paper's PPH-based approach is argued to avoid this failure mode for the text setting
